attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine eye examination. She has high myopia which has been stable for the previous 5 years. She has Ménière's disease and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Visual acuity is 6/6 in both eyes with correction. Dilated fundoscopy reveals areas of lattice degeneration in the peripheral retina of both eyes, without any holes, tears, or detachment.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has asymptomatic lattice degeneration discovered during routine examination. Lattice degeneration is characterised by thinning of the retina in areas and overlying vitreous changes, typically found in the peripheral retina, and occurs in about 6 -8% of people. It is more common in myopic eyes, patients with retinal detachment and hereditary vitreopathies though the overall cause is unknown. Lattice degeneration needs to be monitored for signs of retinal tears and retinal detachment, though the risk of the later may only be between 1- 3%.

Observation with annual review is the most appropriate management for asymptomatic lattice degeneration. The patient should be educated about symptoms of retinal detachment (flashes, floaters, visual field defects) and advised to seek immediate attention if these develop.

Immediate laser photocoagulation would be indicated if there were retinal holes, tears, or specific high-risk features such as family history of retinal detachment or history of detachment in the fellow eye. In asymptomatic lattice degeneration, prophylactic laser has not been shown to reduce the risk of retinal detachment significantly.

Prophylactic scleral buckling is a surgical procedure used to treat retinal detachment, not for prophylaxis in asymptomatic lattice degeneration. It would be excessive and inappropriate in this case.

Urgent referral for vitrectomy would only be necessary if there was an active retinal detachment. This patient has no evidence of retinal detachment or tears.

Topical prednisolone drops have no role in the management of lattice degeneration as this is not an inflammatory condition.
learningPoints: Lattice degeneration is a vitreoretinal condition that can predisposes to retinal tears and detachment.
Prophylactic laser treatment is not routinely recommended for asymptomatic lattice degeneration.
Regular monitoring is appropriate for most patients with lattice degeneration unless specific risk factors are present.
references: Kim T et al. Lattice Degeneration.   American Academy of Ophthalmology EyeWiki 2024. Available at: https://eyewiki.org/Lattice_Degeneration#:~:text=Lattice%20degeneration%20is%20a%20common,retinal%20tears%2C%20and%20retinal%20detachments.

Kim J, Choi K.  Peripheral Lattice Degeneration Imaging with Ultra-Widefield Swept-Source Optical Coherence Tomography.  Korean Journal of Opthalmology.  2023.  Available at: https://www.ekjo.org/m/journal/view.php?number=1831
